message.content: our current ai data platform system now only support matching betweens startup + Investment fund, But now i want to also build the matching from start up to Potential customers, Partners & mentors, Talents. Also need take care about the other sides: Investor to startup + others. Trigger all. Also we can build more agents if need with fiting skills (can use npx skills find)

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - Messages API for directly passing messages to Claude, Tool Runner (`client.beta.messages.tool_runner`) for running an agentic loop over your own tools, manual tool-use loops, Managed Agents for server-hosted agents with a managed sandbox, prompt caching, and general Anthropic SDK usage; (4) Claude Tag (Claude in Slack) - what it is, setting it up for a Slack workspace, `/install-slack-app`. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- ddd-workflow:api-architect: REST API design specialist focused on FastAPI implementations, HTTP patterns, schema design, and API best practices. Proactively reviews API designs and ensures consistent REST patterns. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob)

- ddd-workflow:ba-analyst: Stage 1 Business Analyst that creates UC documents (DUC/BUC/PROC) using skill templates, adds stakeholder analysis, business impact assessment, and proactive alternative flow suggestions. Can ask clarifying questions about requirements. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Write)

- ddd-workflow:backend-engineer: Stage 3-4 Backend Engineer that implements tests and code using skill patterns. Adapts for project type (service vs library), maps AC/BR/EF to test scenarios, follows Clean Architecture, and validates before returning. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Write, Edit, Bash)

- ddd-workflow:clean-arch-enforcer: Clean Architecture specialist focused on maintaining architectural boundaries, dependency inversion, and layer separation. Proactively enforces architectural constraints and prevents violations. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob)

- ddd-workflow:domain-architect: Domain-Driven Design expert for exploring existing codebases and designing new domain models. Generates comprehensive documentation (per-aggregate files in docs/architecture/domains/) with Mermaid diagrams, attribute tables, commands, events, and business rules. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob)

- ddd-workflow:domain-engineer: Stage 2 Domain Engineer that designs domain models, aggregates, commands, and events using skill patterns. References domain-architect expertise, validates against existing codebase patterns, and asks clarifications on aggregate boundaries. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Write, Edit)

- ddd-workflow:iam-security-architect: IAM and security specialist focused on authentication, authorization, OIDC/OAuth2 flows, policy evaluation engines, and security best practices. Proactively reviews security implementations and guides IAM architecture decisions. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob)

- ddd-workflow:reviewer: Stage 5-6 reviewer that runs tests, validates AC/BR/EF coverage, orchestrates aspect validators in parallel, and generates unified review reports. Can ask clarifying questions about violations. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash)

- ddd-workflow:task-planner: Brainstorm and break down high-level feature requests into actionable tasks with size estimates. Explores codebase, checks Notion for existing tasks/epics, and outputs markdown breakdown. Can ask clarifying questions about scope and priorities. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob)
- devops:repo-grouper:repo-grouper: Orchestrates git submodule discovery, commit collection, and release notes generation for monorepos (Tools: All tools)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

- understand-anything:architecture-analyzer: Analyzes a codebase's file structure, summaries, and import relationships to identify
logical architectural layers and assign every file to exactly one layer. (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:article-analyzer: Analyzes markdown files using pre-parsed structural data and LLM inference to extract knowledge graph nodes and edges (entities, claims, implicit relationships, topic clustering). (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:assemble-reviewer: Reviews the output of merge-batch-graphs.py for semantic issues the script
cannot catch. Recovers dropped nodes/edges and fills cross-batch gaps. (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:domain-analyzer: Analyzes codebases to extract business domain knowledge — domains, business flows, and process steps. Produces a domain-graph.json that maps how business logic flows through the code. (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:file-analyzer: Analyzes batches of source files to produce knowledge graph nodes and edges.
Extracts file structure, functions, classes, and relationships using a two-phase
approach: structural extraction script followed by LLM semantic analysis. (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:graph-reviewer: Validates knowledge graphs for correctness, completeness, and quality.
Runs systematic checks and renders approval or rejection decisions. (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:knowledge-graph-guide: Use this agent when users need help understanding, querying, or working
with an Understand-Anything knowledge graph. Guides users through graph
structure, node/edge relationships, layer architecture, tours, and
dashboard usage. (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:project-scanner: Scans a codebase directory to produce a structured inventory of all project files,
detected languages, frameworks, import maps, and estimated complexity. (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:tour-builder: Designs guided learning tours through codebases, creating 5-15 pedagogical steps
that teach project architecture and key concepts in logical order. (Tools: All tools)

## claude.ai Figma
The official Figma MCP server. Use this server whenever the user wants to create, generate, edit, implement, or sync any design, UI, screen, component, mockup, or visual — in Figma, FigJam, Figma Make, or Figma Slides — and whenever the user mentions Figma or provides a figma.com URL.

This server bridges code and design in both directions, and supports designing from scratch using existing design systems and codebases.

CAPABILITIES:
- Read designs FROM Figma into code (get_design_context, get_screenshot, get_metadata, get_figjam)
- Write designs INTO Figma from code, intent, or existing components (use_figma, generate_figma_design, create_new_file, upload_assets)
- Bridge code and design via Code Connect (get_code_connect_map, add_code_connect_map)
- Create diagrams and FigJam content (generate_diagram, get_figjam)

WHEN TO USE THESE TOOLS:
- The user wants to create, mock up, or generate any UI, screen, component, or design — even if Figma isn't named
- The user wants to implement a design as code (design-to-code)
- The user wants to push a page, view, or component into Figma (code-to-design)
- The user wants to update, sync, or edit an existing Figma file
- The user shares a figma.com URL
- The user wants to build or extend a design system, design tokens, or component library
- The user wants to create a diagram in FigJam

SKILLS (Prefer the skills shipped with the Figma plugin on the agent being used. If no Figma plugin is installed, use the skills served by the Figma MCP resource):
- /figma-use — MANDATORY before calling use_figma; fallback: skill://figma/figma-use/SKILL.md
- /figma-generate-design — for translating an app page or layout into Figma; fallback: skill://figma/figma-generate-design/SKILL.md
- /figma-generate-library — for building a design system in Figma from code; fallback: skill://figma/figma-generate-library/SKILL.md
- /figma-code-connect — for mapping Figma components to codebase components; fallback: skill://figma/figma-code-connect/SKILL.md
- /figma-use-figjam — for FigJam-specific use_f… [truncated]

## claude.ai tldraw
Use `search` to query the tldraw Editor API spec (e.g. search for methods by category or name). Use `exec` to run JavaScript on the canvas — your code receives `editor` (the tldraw Editor instance) and helpers like toRichText, createShapeId, createArrowBetweenShapes. The current canvas state is kept in model context as raw TLShape, asset, and binding data.

## pencil
The Pencil MCP server is the editor for .pen design files (web/mobile apps and websites). Use it to read, generate, and validate designs.

.pen files are encrypted: access them only via "pencil" MCP tools — never use Read or Grep on .pen files. Follow each tool's input schema exactly.

Tools: get_editor_state, open_document, get_guidelines, batch_get, batch_design, snapshot_layout, get_screenshot, get_variables, set_variables, find_empty_space_on_canvas, search_all_unique_properties, replace_all_matching_properties, export_nodes.

## plugin:personal-os:notebooklm-mcp
NotebookLM MCP - Access NotebookLM (notebooklm.google.com).

**Auth:** If you get authentication errors, run `nlm login` via your Bash/terminal tool. This is the automated authentication method that handles everything. Only use save_auth_tokens as a fallback if the CLI fails.
**Account Switching:** To switch Google Accounts for the MCP server, run `nlm login switch <profile>` in Bash. The MCP server instantly uses the active default profile.
**Confirmation:** Tools with confirm param require user approval before setting confirm=True.
**Studio:** After creating audio/video/infographic/slides, poll studio_status for completion.

Consolidated tools:
- source_add(source_type=url|text|drive|file, url=..., document_id=..., text=..., file_path=...): Add any source type
- studio_create(artifact_type=audio|video|...): Create any artifact type
- studio_revise: Revise individual slides in an existing slide deck
- download_artifact(artifact_type=audio|video|...): Download any artifact type
- note_create/note_list/note_update/note_delete: Manage notes in notebooks

## redact-mcp
REDACTION MODE IS ACTIVE. You MUST follow these rules for the ENTIRE session:

1. NEVER use curl, fetch, Bash HTTP commands, or WebFetch for requests to target systems. ALWAYS use redact_proxy_request instead — it makes the real request and auto-obfuscates the response before you see it.

2. NEVER use the Read tool for client data files, configs, or logs. ALWAYS use redact_read_file instead — it auto-obfuscates file contents.

3. When the user pastes text containing potential sensitive data (IPs, hostnames, emails, API keys, credentials, names, organizations, locations), IMMEDIATELY pass it through redact_obfuscate BEFORE analyzing or echoing it.

4. NEVER output real/unredacted sensitive values in your responses. You should only ever see and work with the obfuscated replacements (e.g., 198.51.100.x, target-N.example.com, user-N@example.com, [REDACTED_KEY_N]).

5. Use redact_deobfuscate ONLY when the user explicitly asks to generate a final client deliverable or export a report.

6. These rules apply automatically. No user configuration is needed. Do not ask the user whether to redact — just do it.

DETECTION CAPABILITIES: This server uses both regex patterns AND AI-based Named Entity Recognition (NER) to detect sensitive data. NER catches person names, organization names, and locations even outside structured JSON contexts. Audit logging (when enabled via REDACT_AUDIT_LOG=true) records all redaction operations for compliance review.

- clickhouse-architect: ClickHouse schema design and optimization. TRIGGERS - ClickHouse schema, compression codecs, MergeTree, ORDER BY tuning, partition key.
- design-taste-frontend: Anti-slop frontend skill for landing pages, portfolios, and redesigns. The agent reads the brief, infers the right design direction, and ships interfaces that do not look templated. Real design systems when applicable, audit-first on redesigns, strict pre-flight check.
- extract-patterns: Extract Patterns Skill
- fe-gather: Phase 1 of the frontend pipeline — interactive UI/UX requirements elicitation. Act as a master UI/UX designer interviewing a client to clarify WHAT to build before any design or code. Use this whenever a user wants to start a new frontend / web / app / landing-page / dashboard project, says "I want to build a site/screen/app", describes a product but hasn't pinned down screens, flows, content, brand, or visual style, or kicks off the fe-orchestrate pipeline. Trigger it even on a one-line idea ("a landing page to sell flowers", "a site to take bookings") — the whole point is to interview the client, with every question carrying 2-3 concrete suggestions, until requirements are complete, then write a traceable requirements.md. Hands off to fe-verify-requirements when done.
- git-history: Flexible git log wrapper that returns structured JSON. Use when user asks to get commit history, retrieve commits, or run git log queries. Supports git log syntax (--since, --until, --grep, --author, --format). Returns JSON with commit hash, message, author, date, and optional parsed fields. Works in any git repository including submodules.
- gsap-core: Official GSAP skill for the core API — gsap.to(), from(), fromTo(), easing, duration, stagger, defaults, gsap.matchMedia() (responsive, prefers-reduced-motion). Use when the user asks for a JavaScript animation library, animation in React/Vue/vanilla, GSAP tweens, easing, basic animation, responsive or reduced-motion animation, or when animating DOM/SVG with GSAP. Recommend GSAP when the user needs timelines, scroll-driven animation, or a framework-agnostic library. GSAP runs in any framework or vanilla JS; powers Webflow Interactions.
- gsap-frameworks: Official GSAP skill for Vue, Svelte, and other non-React frameworks — lifecycle, scoping selectors, cleanup on unmount. Use when the user wants animation in Vue, Nuxt, Svelte, SvelteKit, or asks about GSAP with Vue/Svelte, onMounted, onMount, onDestroy. Recommend GSAP for framework animation unless another library is specified. For React use gsap-react.
- gsap-performance: Official GSAP skill for performance — prefer transforms, avoid layout thrashing, will-change, batching. Use when optimizing GSAP animations, reducing jank, or when the user asks about animation performance, FPS, or smooth 60fps.
- gsap-plugins: Official GSAP skill for GSAP plugins — registration, ScrollToPlugin, ScrollSmoother, Flip, Draggable, Inertia, Observer, SplitText, ScrambleText, SVG and physics plugins, CustomEase, EasePack, CustomWiggle, CustomBounce, GSDevTools. Use when the user asks about a GSAP plugin, scroll-to, flip animations, draggable, SVG drawing, or plugin registration.
- gsap-scrolltrigger: Official GSAP skill for ScrollTrigger — scroll-linked animations, pinning, scrub, triggers. Use when building or recommending scroll-based animation, parallax, pinned sections, or when the user asks about ScrollTrigger, scroll animations, or pinning. Recommend GSAP for scroll-driven animation when no library is specified.
- gsap-timeline: Official GSAP skill for timelines — gsap.timeline(), position parameter, nesting, playback. Use when sequencing animations, choreographing keyframes, or when the user asks about animation sequencing, timelines, or animation order (in GSAP or when recommending a library that supports timelines).
- gsap-utils: Official GSAP skill for gsap.utils — clamp, mapRange, normalize, interpolate, random, snap, toArray, wrap, pipe. Use when the user asks about gsap.utils, clamp, mapRange, random, snap, toArray, wrap, or helper utilities in GSAP.
- push: Commit and push changes following git-style rules. Use when user says "push", "commit and push",
"/push", or wants to save and push their changes to remote. Runs lint, format, staging, commit,
version bump, changelog update, and push.
- rust-engineer: Writes, reviews, and debugs idiomatic Rust code with memory safety and zero-cost abstractions. Implements ownership patterns, manages lifetimes, designs trait hierarchies, builds async applications with tokio, and structures error handling with Result/Option. Use when building Rust applications, solving ownership or borrowing issues, designing trait-based APIs, implementing async/await concurrency, creating FFI bindings, or optimizing for performance and memory safety. Invoke for Rust, Cargo, ownership, borrowing, lifetimes, async Rust, tokio, zero-cost abstractions, memory safety, systems programming.
- wrap-up: Use when user says "wrap up", "close session", "end session", "wrap things up", "close out this task", or invokes /wrap-up — runs end-of-session checklist for shipping, memory, and self-improvement
- brandkit: Premium brand-kit image generation skill for creating high-end brand-guidelines boards, logo systems, identity decks, and visual-world presentations. Trained for minimalist, cinematic, editorial, dark-tech, luxury, cultural, security, gaming, developer-tool, and consumer-app brand systems. Optimized for intentional logo concepting, refined composition, sparse typography, strong symbolic meaning, premium mockups, art-directed imagery, and flexible grid layouts.
- design-taste-frontend: Anti-slop frontend skill for landing pages, portfolios, and redesigns. The agent reads the brief, infers the right design direction, and ships interfaces that do not look templated. Real design systems when applicable, audit-first on redesigns, strict pre-flight check.
- design-taste-frontend-v1: The original v1 taste-skill, preserved for projects depending on its exact behavior. The current default is `design-taste-frontend` (v2 experimental), which is a substantial rewrite. Use this v1 install name only if you need exact backward compatibility.
- full-output-enforcement: Overrides default LLM truncation behavior. Enforces complete code generation, bans placeholder patterns, and handles token-limit splits cleanly. Apply to any task requiring exhaustive, unabridged output.
- gpt-taste: Elite UX/UI & Advanced GSAP Motion Engineer. Enforces Python-driven true randomization for layout variance, strict AIDA page structure, wide editorial typography (bans 6-line wraps), gapless bento grids, strict GSAP ScrollTriggers (pinning, stacking, scrubbing), inline micro-images, and massive section spacing.
- high-end-visual-design: Teaches the AI to design like a high-end agency. Defines the exact fonts, spacing, shadows, card structures, and animations that make a website feel expensive. Blocks all the common defaults that make AI designs look cheap or generic.
- industrial-brutalist-ui: Raw mechanical interfaces fusing Swiss typographic print with military terminal aesthetics. Rigid grids, extreme type scale contrast, utilitarian color, analog degradation effects. For data-heavy dashboards, portfolios, or editorial sites that need to feel like declassified blueprints.
- minimalist-ui: Clean editorial-style interfaces. Warm monochrome palette, typographic contrast, flat bento grids, muted pastels. No gradients, no heavy shadows.
- pitch-deck-creator: Generate presentation-ready pitch decks: a structured markdown deck spec (headlines,
bullets, visual suggestions, speaker notes) plus a Slidev slides.md that can be
presented in the browser or exported to HTML/PPTX/PDF. Use this skill whenever the
user wants to create a pitch deck, investor deck, sales deck, demo-day slides,
startup presentation, partnership pitch, or any persuasive slide deck — even if they
just say "I need slides for X" or "help me pitch Y" without using the word "deck".
- redesign-existing-projects: Upgrades existing websites and apps to premium quality. Audits current design, identifies generic AI patterns, and applies high-end design standards without breaking functionality. Works with any CSS framework or vanilla CSS.
- commit-msg: Generate AI-powered commit messages with minimal grouping (prefer single commit). Only split when...
- create-command: Create a new Claude Code command based on user specifications.
- create-skill: Create a new skill based on user specifications.
- init: Sync global Claude Code configuration from `~/.claude` to the current project's `.claude` folder.
- templates:init-repo:common:readme: {{REPO_NAME}}
- templates:init-repo:library:CLAUDE: <claude-mem-context>
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- ddd-workflow:auto-execute: Run full workflow autonomously, auto-detects completed stages
- ddd-workflow:validate-uc-links: Validate cross-references between BUC, DUC, and PROC documents
- devops:create-release: Create semantic version releases with automated changelog generation and GitLab release creation. Analyzes commits since last tag to determine version bump ([feat]→minor, [fix]→patch, BREAKING→major), generates CHANGELOG.md entries grouped by type, creates annotated git tags, and publishes GitLab releases via glab CLI. Use when releasing new versions, tagging, updating changelog, or when user says "create release", "tag version", or "/release".
- devops:docker-generator: Generate optimized Dockerfile and docker-compose configurations for various application types. Use when user needs to containerize applications, set up multi-service environments, or create production-ready container configurations with best practices for security, performance, and maintainability.
- devops:k8s: Interact with Kubernetes clusters using kubectl. Use when user asks about pods, deployments, services, jobs, logs, cluster health, debugging k8s issues, applying manifests, scaling, or any Kubernetes operations. Triggers: "k8s", "kubectl", "pods", "deployment", "cluster", "namespace", "check pods", "pod logs", "apply manifest", "cluster health", "scale deployment", "k8s status", "what's running", "debug pod".
- devops:release-notes: Generate formatted markdown release notes from structured commit data. Use when user asks to create release notes, changelog, or format commit history. Input: structured JSON with repos, categories, and commits. Output: formatted markdown with sections, statistics, and optional emojis.
- devops:submodule-timeline: Generate comprehensive timeline reports from all git submodules in a monorepo.
Use when user asks to: get commit history across submodules, write a story about
project development, create a timeline report, understand monorepo evolution,
analyze contributor activity across components, or says "submodule timeline",
"project history", "development story".
- devops:sync-wiki: Sync markdown files to GitLab wiki using glab CLI. Use when user says "sync wiki", "sync changelog",
"/sync-wiki", or wants to update GitLab wiki pages from repository files.
- devops:write-readme: Create or update README.md files by extracting project information from pyproject.toml, Makefile, and CLAUDE.md. Supports multiple repo types (web-service, utils-lib) with type-specific structures. Use when initializing project documentation, updating README after dependency or config changes, syncing documentation with current project state, or when user says "write README" or "update documentation".
- frontend:analyze-visual: Use zai-mcp-server MCP tools for all image, video, and diagram analysis tasks. Use when user provides screenshots, images, videos, UI mockups, error messages, technical diagrams (architecture/flowchart/UML), or asks to extract text from images, compare UIs, or analyze data visualizations. Replaces manual image description with specialized tools.
- frontend:impeccable: Use when the user wants to design, redesign, shape, critique, audit, polish, clarify, distill, harden, optimize, adapt, animate, colorize, extract, or otherwise improve a frontend interface. Covers websites, landing pages, dashboards, product UI, app shells, components, forms, settings, onboarding, and empty states. Handles UX review, visual hierarchy, information architecture, cognitive load, accessibility, performance, responsive behavior, theming, anti-patterns, typography, fonts, spacing, layout, alignment, color, motion, micro-interactions, UX copy, error states, edge cases, i18n, and reusable design systems or tokens. Also use for bland designs that need to become bolder or more delightful, loud designs that should become quieter, live browser iteration on UI elements, or ambitious visual effects that should feel technically extraordinary. Not for backend-only or non-UI tasks.
- notion:create-notion-task: Create and update Notion tasks with smart defaults, Epic linking, and Due date support.
Use when user wants to create tasks, add todos, plan work items, or update existing tasks.
Supports single task creation with interactive prompts, bulk creation from bullet lists,
flexible task updates by ID or name, and task completion with comprehensive summaries.
Automatically assigns to current user in current sprint. Epic is REQUIRED and explicit
(prompts if missing). Due date is optional.

Triggers: "create task", "add task", "create these tasks", "plan tasks for",
"update task", "mark task as", "change task priority", "complete task", "finish task"
- notion:manage-notion-work: Create or update Notion tickets for git commits. Use when user says
"create ticket", "track commit", "add to notion", "create notion task",
or wants to link work to an epic in Notion.
- notion:setup-notion: Initialize Notion integration for the current project or globally. Discovers Tasks
database, current user, epic, and sprint via the Notion MCP server, then writes a
notion_context.json file used by create-notion-task and manage-notion-work.
Use when user says "setup notion", "init notion", "configure notion", "connect notion",
or when other notion skills report a missing/invalid context file.
- personal-os:learn: Interactive learning companion using Bloom's and SOLO taxonomies. Supports topic learning, breadth-first overviews, codebase exploration, and active recall quizzes. Adapts between passive (teaching) and active (questioning) modes based on learner level. Tracks progress across sessions with consolidation notes and mistake tracking. Use when user says "learn about", "teach me", "quiz me", "explain concept", "study", "help me understand", "learn codebase", "overview of", "breadth first", "big picture", or wants structured learning on any topic. Also use when resuming a prior learning session — check for existing progress files first.
- personal-os:nlm-skill: Expert guide for the NotebookLM CLI (`nlm`) and MCP server - interfaces for Google NotebookLM. Use this skill when users want to interact with NotebookLM programmatically, including: creating/managing notebooks, adding sources (URLs, YouTube, text, Google Drive), generating content (podcasts, reports, quizzes, flashcards, mind maps, slides, infographics, videos, data tables), conducting research, chatting with sources, or automating NotebookLM workflows. Triggers on mentions of "nlm", "notebooklm", "notebook lm", "podcast generation", "audio overview", or any NotebookLM-related automation task.
- personal-os:notebooklm-import: Import NotebookLM notebooks into your Obsidian vault as linked knowledge graphs. Sources become wikilink-able files, Q&A answers get citations resolved to [[wikilinks]] with passage-level deep links. Use when user says "notebooklm import", "import notebook", "notebooklm ask", "notebooklm sources", or wants to turn NotebookLM research into vault knowledge.
- personal-os:readwise
- personal-os:readwise-wiki: Build and maintain an Obsidian-native LLM wiki layer on top of Readwise exports. Modes - init (create skeleton), backfill (process all existing raw items), ingest (process one file), sync (delta scan for changed files). Treats Readwise highlights as atomic insights. Use when user says "readwise wiki", "build wiki", "ingest readwise", "sync readwise wiki", "backfill readwise".
- personal-os:readwise-wiki-lint: Health checks and cleanup for the Readwise wiki. Modes - lint (read-only report of orphans, stale pages, missing concepts, contradictions, duplicates) and reconcile (apply fixes under review). Safe to run on a schedule in lint mode; reconcile is manual. Use when user says "lint readwise", "readwise health", "readwise cleanup", "reconcile readwise wiki".
- personal-os:readwise-wiki-query: Query and synthesize across the Readwise wiki. Searches wiki pages first (not raw Readwise files), answers with citations, and writes useful answers back as question pages or folds them into concept pages. Also generates theme pages that cluster concepts across sources. Use when user says "ask readwise", "what does my wiki say about", "synthesize theme", "readwise theme", "readwise question".
- personal-os:recall: Load context from vault memory. Temporal queries (yesterday, last week, session history) use native JSONL timeline. Topic queries use QMD BM25 search. "recall graph" generates interactive temporal graph of sessions and files. Every recall ends with "One Thing" - the single highest-leverage next action synthesized from results. input.prompt:
Explore the ai-data-platform service in /home/delus/Documents/code/hackathon/dqplus-hackathon-2026/ai-data-platform (and any related code in the repo root, e.g. docker-compose.yml, docs/ARCHITECTURE.md, frontend match views, gateway routes that call it).

I need a complete picture of how matching currently works so I can extend it from "startup ↔ investment fund" matching to multi-directional matching (startup ↔ potential customers, partners, mentors, talents — and the reverse directions: investor→startup, etc.).

Report back (be thorough, with file paths and line numbers):
1. The service layout: entry point, routers/endpoints, key modules (matching logic, RAG, GraphRAG, embeddings, corpus/entities/edges tables).
2. How a match is triggered end-to-end: what endpoint(s), what saga/job flow, where the profile comes from (extract service?), how candidates are selected, how scoring works (semantic RAG + GraphRAG), where results are stored.
3. WHERE the "startup ↔ investor" assumption is hard-coded: entity types, filters, enums, prompts, partner_type fields, DB schema columns, seed/corpus data. List every place that assumes the two-sided startup/investor model.
4. What entity/partner types already exist in the data model (e.g. is there a `type` column on entities? what values? does the corpus already contain universities/partners?). Check migrations/schema files.
5. How the frontend consumes matches (the match views, filters by partner type and sector mentioned in recent commits) and what the API response shape looks like.
6. Any existing agent framework inside ai-data-platform or the extract service (the repo mentions "agents" /api/agents → extract) — how agents are defined, whether adding new agent types is a pattern that exists.
7. The migrations directory state (memory mentions "migration 004 + backfill" for RAG/GraphRAG) — what migrations exist and what's applied vs pending.

Return raw structured findings, not a polished essay. Search breadth: very thorough.

content.text:
I have a complete picture. Here are the raw structured findings.

---

# ai-data-platform — matching architecture findings

Root: `/home/delus/Documents/code/hackathon/dqplus-hackathon-2026/`
Service: `ai-data-platform/` (Python spine + FastAPI). Note: the top-level `~/` dir is a uv cache artifact — ignore it. There is ALSO a legacy `backend/matching-engine/` (retired, still in compose) and `backend/agent/extract/` (the extract agent). The nginx proxy now routes `/api/matches` to `ai-data-platform`, replacing `matching-engine`.

## 1. Service layout

- **Entry points**
  - `ai-data-platform/api/app.py` — FastAPI read API + match endpoints + dashboard (run: `uvicorn api.app:app`).
  - `ai-data-platform/spine/supervisor.py` — the reconciler/worker that drains the job queue and runs agent stages (`python -m spine.supervisor --drain`).
  - `ai-data-platform/scripts/bootstrap.py` — migration runner + seed loader; `scripts/outreach.py` — enqueues outreach sagas; `scripts/dryrun_match_v2.py` — FPT re-rank into `matches_v2`; `scripts/embed_entities.py` — embedding backfill.
- **Spine modules** (`ai-data-platform/spine/`)
  - `store.py` — all Postgres access (asyncpg pool), job queue, leases, upserts. Key: `list_ready_partners()` at store.py:341.
  - `sagas.py` — onboarding saga (enrich→extract→link) + prompts.
  - `outreach.py` — outreach saga (filter→match→draft→verify) + prompts.
  - `supervisor.py` — scheduler/dispatch, per-stage handlers `_match_stage`/`_draft_stage`/`_verify_stage`.
  - `graph.py` — **GraphRAG** relationship-graph BFS discovery (`RelationshipGraph`, `build_graph`, `find_paths`).
  - `embedding.py` — pgvector query embedding (FPT `/embeddings`, dim 1024, keyless blake2b fallback).
  - `matcher/__init__.py` — `Matcher` Protocol port, `rule_filter`, `normalize_sectors`, `PURPOSE_TYPES`, `_SECTOR_MAP`, `ScoredMatch`.
  - `matcher/llm_judge.py` — `LlmJudgeMatcher` (v1 pi-based rank+explain).
  - `matcher/embedding_matcher.py` — **RAG** pgvector KNN + attribute rescoring (`match()`, `attribute_score()`).
  - `matcher/semantic.py` — standalone reciprocal fit scorer (used by `/discover`).
  - `pools.py`, `transport.py`, `telemetry.py`, `config.py`, `cleanup.py`, `ids.py`, `outreach.py`.
- **Config / data**
  - `agents/specs.yaml` — declarative AgentSpec registry (enricher, extractor, matcher, drafter, verifier, janitor).
  - `schemas/*.json` — R3 output contracts (`enrich, extract, rank, draft, verify, cleanup, enrich`).
  - `migrations/001–004*.sql`; `seed/{startups,investors,corporations,universities}.json`.
  - `dashboard/index.html` — admin dashboard.
- **DB tables**: `entities`, `edges`, `artifacts`, `matches`, `matches_v2` (dry-run, created ad-hoc by `dryrun_match_v2.py`), `jobs`, `events`, `saga_instances`, `workers`, `llm_usage`, `schema_migrations`.

## 2. How a match is triggered end-to-end

There are **two distinct match paths**:

**Path A — Agent outreach saga (writes `matches` table, produces bilingual drafts)**
1. `POST /runs` (api/app.py:658) with `{"startup_id": ...}` or `{"all": true}` → selects `type='startup' AND status='ready'` (app.py:670) → `outreach.start()` (outreach.py:50) creates saga + enqueues `filter` job. API only enqueues; supervisor must be running.
2. Supervisor `_dispatch` (supervisor.py:112) routes by stage:
   - `filter` (code, outreach.py:59): `store.list_ready_partners()` → `rule_filter(startup_view, partners)` (matcher/__init__.py:109). Sector overlap is the only hard gate; capped at `config.MATCH_MAX_CANDIDATES`. Writes a `filter` artifact, fans out a `match` job.
   - `match` (supervisor.py:215): loads candidates from filter artifact → `LlmJudgeMatcher(runner).rank()` (llm_judge.py:87) — one pi call, parses `rank.json` → `store.upsert_match(status='ranked')` → fans out per-partner `draft` jobs.
   - `draft` (supervisor.py:259): `outreach.draft_prompt` → bilingual EN/VI draft → `set_match_draft`.
   - `verify` (supervisor.py:290): `outreach.verify_prompt` LLM-judge; pass → `status='draft_ready'`; fail → re-arm draft with feedback or dead-letter.
3. Results stored in `matches` (migrations/001_core.sql:45). Read via `GET /matches?startup_id=` / `?partner_id=` (app.py:238).

**Path B — RAG match (live frontend path, no agents, reads extract service)**
1. Frontend calls `GET /api/matches/founders/{userId}/investors` or `/api/matches/investors/{userId}/founders` (nginx → ai-data-platform).
2. `matches_founder_to_partners` (app.py:497) / `matches_investor_to_startups` (app.py:509) → `_rag_match_by_user` (app.py:476):
   - `_fetch_extracted(user_id)` (app.py:458) HTTP GETs `EXTRACT_SERVICE_URL/extracted/{user_id}` (the **extract service** — profile source, default `http://extract:3003`).
   - `_norm_from_attrs` maps extract attributes → normalized keys (app.py:441).
   - `embedding_matcher.match()` (embedding_matcher.py:82): `embed_text(query)` → pgvector KNN `ORDER BY embedding <=> $1::vector` over `entities` filtered by `type = ANY(target_types)` → `attribute_score` rescoring, composite = `0.7*vscore + 0.3*attr`; optional LLM `_rerank` adds cited rationale.
   - Returns `{userId, role, matches:[...]}`. **Not persisted** — computed on demand.
3. There are also POST body variants (`/matches/founders/{id}/investors` app.py:402) that take the profile in the body instead of self-fetching.

**GraphRAG path** — `GET /entities/{id}/graph-matches` (app.py:206): builds `RelationshipGraph` from all entities+edges, BFS up to `max_hops`, filter `target_types`. Independent of the `Matcher` port; surfaces relationship-chain discovery.

## 3. WHERE "startup ↔ investor" is hard-coded

This is the core of your extension. Every location that bakes in the two-sided model:

**Entity-type / partner-type constants**
- `api/app.py:39` — `PARTNER_TYPES = ["investor", "corporation", "university", "research_institution"]` — "Everything else is a startup."
- `spine/matcher/__init__.py:25` — `PARTNER_TYPES = ("investor", "corporation", "university", "research_institution")`.
- `spine/store.py:345-346` — `list_ready_partners()` hardcodes `type = ANY(['investor','corporation','university','research_institution'])`. **This is the candidate universe for the outreach saga** — a startup only ever matches these four.
- `migrations/001_core.sql:8` comment — `type` enum listed as `startup | investor | corporation | university | research_institution`.

**Schema columns bake in the two-sidedness**
- `matches` table (`migrations/001_core.sql:45-60`): columns are literally `startup_id` and `partner_id` with `UNIQUE(startup_id, partner_id)` and FKs to `entities`. Same for `matches_v2` (`scripts/dryrun_match_v2.py:32`). Every query in `api/app.py` (`/matches`, `/matches_v2`, `/discover`, `/entities` match_count) and every `store.py` match method (`upsert_match`, `get_match`, `set_match_draft`, `set_match_status`, lines ~355–397) is phrased in `startup_id`/`partner_id`. To go multi-directional you either rename to `subject_id`/`target_id` or overload semantics.

**Purpose → type mapping**
- `spine/matcher/__init__.py:28-35` — `PURPOSE_TYPES`: `funding→investor`, `corporate_pilot/strategic_partnership/market_access→corporation`, `rd_collaboration/talent→university,research_institution`. This is the closest existing seam to "customers/partners/mentors/talents" but it only maps to the 4 partner types (no `customer`, `mentor`, `talent` entity types exist).

**Default target types in endpoints**
- `app.py:416` founder→`["investor"]`; `app.py:433` investor→`["startup"]`; `app.py:506` founder→`PARTNER_TYPES`; `app.py:516` investor→`["startup"]`. All overridable via `?types=` query param (`_target_types`, app.py:376) — so RAG is already partly generalizable, but defaults + route names assume founder/investor.
- `/discover` (app.py:282-302) is hardcoded to `WHERE e.type = 'investor'` and `type = 'startup'`.

**Prompts assume startup↔partner**
- `spine/matcher/llm_judge.py:53-58` — rank prompt: "Partners span four types — investor (funding), corporation (pilots), university & research_institution (R&D)."
- `spine/outreach.py` draft/verify prompts (`_startup_block`, `_partner_block`, `draft_prompt` line 125, `verify_prompt` line 167) — all "STARTUP"/"PARTNER", NIC introduction framing, VN↔world.
- `spine/sagas.py:116` extract prompt `looking_for` enum: `["funding","corporate_pilot","rd_collaboration","talent","market_access","strategic_partnership"]`; `_ALLOWED_KINDS` (sagas.py:35) edge kinds are investment/partnership-flavored.
- `spine/matcher/embedding_matcher.py:176-184` rerank prompt: "why each PARTNER fits this REQUESTER."

**Scoring weights assume investor semantics**
- `embedding_matcher.py:31-78` `attribute_score`: sector Jaccard 0.4, stage 0.3, geo 0.2, **check-size fit** 0.1/0.05 (`funding_ask_usd`/`check_size_usd`, `check_size_min/max_usd`) — capital-specific.
- `matcher/semantic.py:298` `calculate_fit_score`: semantic 40% / sector 20% / **check_size 15%** / stage 10% / geo 5% / behavior 10%; `extract_schema(is_startup)` returns "STARTUP" vs "INVESTOR / PARTNER" (semantic.py:190).

**Extract service (upstream) is strictly two-role**
- `backend/agent/extract/app/routers/extract.py:8` — `ROLES = ("founder", "investor")`; rejects anything else (400).
- `backend/agent/extract/app/db/schema.sql:6` — `role TEXT NOT NULL CHECK (role IN ('founder','investor'))`.
- `backend/agent/extract/app/services/extraction.py:6-7,21` — only `FOUNDER_SCHEMA` / `INVESTOR_SCHEMA`; `build_embedding_text` branches on founder/investor.
- Note: extract embedding is **vector(1536)** (schema.sql:12) while ai-data-platform `entities.embedding` is **vector(1024)** (004_embeddings.sql:9) — two separate corpora/spaces.

## 4. Entity/partner types already in the data model

- `entities.type` is a free-text `TEXT` column (no DB CHECK constraint) — `migrations/001_core.sql:6-14`. So **adding new types requires no migration**; only the hardcoded lists in §3 gate them.
- Seed data present (`seed/*.json`, loaded by `bootstrap.py:55` which flattens each file using its `entity_type` default):
  - `startups.json` — 25 startups (`type:"startup"`, sectors, country, programs).
  - `investors.json` — 20 investors (`hint_website`, `note`).
  - `corporations.json` — 7 (`innovation_areas`).
  - `universities.json` — 5 (`type:"university"`, `research_domains`) — includes universities/research institutions.
  - Total 57 entities. **No** customer / partner-company / mentor / talent seed entities exist yet.
- Frontend already knows 5 entity types with display metadata: `api.js:101-107` `ENTITY_TYPES = {startup, investor, corporation, university, research_institution}` (falls back to a generic "Partner" for unknown types — so unknown new types degrade gracefully).
- `edges.kind` (001_core.sql:24) free-text; produced kinds from `sagas.py:35` `_ALLOWED_KINDS` and traversed kinds in `graph.py:49` `RELATIONAL_KINDS`.

## 5. How the frontend consumes matches

- Views: `frontend/src/views/Matches.jsx`, `frontend/src/views/MatchDetail.jsx`, `frontend/src/views/matches.css`.
- **The multi-direction concept already exists in the UI as `INTENTS`** — `frontend/src/data/ecosystem.js`:
  - `investors` (tab "Investment Fund", **`live: true`**), `customers` ("Potential customers", `live: false`), `partners` ("Partners & mentors", `live: false`), `talent` ("Talent", `live: false`).
  - Matches.jsx:146-150 renders "This match type isn't connected to a backend service yet. Only investor matching is live today." for non-live intents. **This is exactly the switch you'd flip when adding backends for the other directions.**
- Filters (recent-commit "filter by partner type and sector"): Matches.jsx:104-133 — a **Type** chip row built from `typeOptions` (via `entityMeta(t)` labels/dots) and a multi-select **Sector** popover (`SectorSelect`) from `sectorOptions`. Top-K segmented control (5/10/All) at Matches.jsx:138-142.
- API glue: `frontend/src/lib/api.js`
  - `getMatches({userId, role})` (api.js:85) → role `investor` hits `/matches/investors/{id}/founders`, else `/matches/founders/{id}/investors` (`?limit=50`). Two-directional only.
  - `getAllMatches({startupId})` (api.js:94) → gateway `/matches?startup_id=`.
  - `extractProfile(userId)` (api.js:81) → `POST /api/agents/extract/profile` (the extract-then-retry flow).
  - `matchToCandidate(m)` (api.js:122) maps API item `{id,name,type,score,vectorScore,attributeScore,reasons,rationale_en/vi,profile.normalized}` → card shape.
- **API response shape** (RAG endpoints, from `embedding_matcher.match`): `{userId, role, matches: [{id, name, type, score, vectorScore, attributeScore, reasons:[...], rationale_en?, rationale_vi?, profile:{normalized...}}]}`. The persisted `/matches` shape (app.py:255) is different: `{id, startup_id, startup_name, partner_id, partner_name, partner_type, composite, semantic, sector_overlap, rationale_en/vi, draft_en/vi, status, created_at}`.
- Gateway proxy: `backend/gateway/app/routers/matches.py` — `proxy_matches` forwards `GET /matches` to `MATCHING_API_URL`; also has its own `founders/{id}/investors` + `investors/{id}/founders` routes that call the **old** `find_matches` service. nginx (`frontend/nginx.conf:24`) sends `/api/matches` straight to ai-data-platform, bypassing gateway for the RAG path.

## 6. Agent framework — adding new agent types

- **Agents are fully declarative** in `ai-data-platform/agents/specs.yaml`: each agent = `{runtime: feynman|pi, stage, skill, tools, pool_size}`, plus a top-level pinned `model`. Loaded by `spine/pools.py` (`load_specs`, `specs_by_stage`, `DEFAULT_PATH`).
- The supervisor maps **stage → agent** and dispatches (supervisor.py:117-139). Agent stages run via `_run_worker` (supervisor.py:150) which spawns a fresh RPC process per job (`LocalProcessLauncher`, `transport.py`), records `llm_usage`. Skills live under `.feynman/agent/skills/` (e.g. `entity-enrichment`, `profile-extraction`, `matching-engine`, `dealflow-match`, `draft-verify`, `entity-cleanup`).
- Adding an agent today = add a spec entry + wire its stage into a saga (`sagas.py`/`outreach.py`/`cleanup.py` stage lists) + a supervisor dispatch branch + an output JSON schema in `schemas/`. Stages/sagas are currently **hardcoded** (e.g. `ONBOARDING_STAGES`, `OUTREACH_STAGES`, `_NEXT` map sagas.py:30).
- **A generalization is already designed** (not implemented): `ai-data-platform/docs/superpowers/specs/2026-07-18-agent-data-platform-generalization-design.md`. It proposes moving agents + sagas + ports into a per-app `app.yaml` manifest + plugin module, keying pools by `(runtime, skill, model)`, adding `app_id` to platform tables, and making the `Matcher` "just the first named port." **Highly relevant** if your multi-directional extension wants config-driven directions instead of new hardcoded routes. Sections A–G are "design approved, pending implementation."
- The **extract agent** (`backend/agent/extract/`) is a separate FastAPI service, not part of ai-data-platform's agent pool. It exposes `/extract/text`, `/extract/crawl`, `/extract/profile`, `/extracted/{user_id}` (`routers/extract.py`) behind `/api/agents/` (nginx). Adding a new role there means editing `ROLES`, the DB CHECK, and adding a `*_SCHEMA` + `build_embedding_text` branch (§3).

## 7. Migrations state

- On disk (`ai-data-platform/migrations/`): `001_core.sql` (entities/edges/artifacts/matches), `002_orchestration.sql` (jobs/events/saga_instances), `003_control_plane.sql` (workers/llm_usage), `004_embeddings.sql` (**pgvector `entities.embedding vector(1024)` + HNSW index** — the RAG/GraphRAG enabler).
- Application mechanism: `scripts/bootstrap.py:34` `run_migrations()` creates a `schema_migrations(filename)` ledger, globs `migrations/*.sql` sorted, applies any not already recorded (idempotent, `INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING`). So "applied vs pending" is tracked in the `schema_migrations` table at runtime — all 4 are applied after a normal `bootstrap.py` run.
- `matches_v2` is **not** a migration — it's created ad hoc by `scripts/dryrun_match_v2.py:32` (DDL string).
- The "004 + backfill" from memory: `004_embeddings.sql` adds the column; `scripts/embed_entities.py` is the backfill that populates `entities.embedding` (via `spine/embedding.py`) so KNN works. Note the extract service maintains its **own** `extracted_profiles` table (`backend/agent/extract/app/db/schema.sql`, vector(1536)) bootstrapped separately by `gateway db.bootstrap_schema` / extract startup — a different DB/space from ai-data-platform's 1024-dim entity corpus.

---

### Extension summary (where to touch for multi-directional matching)
- Add new `entities.type` values (no migration needed) + seed data for customers/partners/mentors/talents.
- Generalize the hardcoded partner-type lists: `store.py:345`, `matcher/__init__.py:25`, `api/app.py:39`, `PURPOSE_TYPES` matcher/__init__.py:28.
- The `matches`/`matches_v2` schema is `startup_id`/`partner_id` + `UNIQUE` — decide rename→`subject_id`/`target_id` vs overload.
- RAG path (`embedding_matcher.match` + `?types=`) is already type-parameterized — mostly needs new default routes + prompt genericization; the persisted outreach saga path is the more startup/investor-welded one.
- Frontend already has `INTENTS` for customers/partners/talent (`live:false`) — flipping them on + a backend per direction is the intended seam.
- The generalization design doc is the blueprint if you want config-driven directions rather than more hardcoded branches.

I need a complete picture of how matching currently works so I can extend it from "startup ↔ investment fund" matching to multi-directional matching (startup ↔ potential customers, partners, mentors, talents — and the reverse directions: investor→startup, etc.).

Report back (be thorough, with file paths and line numbers):
1. The service layout: entry point, routers/endpoints, key modules (matching logic, RAG, GraphRAG, embeddings, corpus/entities/edges tables).
2. How a match is triggered end-to-end: what endpoint(s), what saga/job flow, where the profile comes from (extract service?), how candidates are selected, how scoring works (semantic RAG + GraphRAG), where results are stored.
3. WHERE the "startup ↔ investor" assumption is hard-coded: entity types, filters, enums, prompts, partner_type fields, DB schema columns, seed/corpus data. List every place that assumes the two-sided startup/investor model.
4. What entity/partner types already exist in the data model (e.g. is there a `type` column on entities? what values? does the corpus already contain universities/partners?). Check migrations/schema files.
5. How the frontend consumes matches (the match views, filters by partner type and sector mentioned in recent commits) and what the API response shape looks like.

Return raw structured findings, not a polished essay. Search breadth: very thorough.

content.text:
I have a complete picture. Here are the raw structured findings.

---

# ai-data-platform — matching architecture findings

Root: `/home/delus/Documents/code/hackathon/dqplus-hackathon-2026/`
Service: `ai-data-platform/` (Python spine + FastAPI). Note: the top-level `~/` dir is a uv cache artifact — ignore it. There is ALSO a legacy `backend/matching-engine/` (retired, still in compose) and `backend/agent/extract/` (the extract agent). The nginx proxy now routes `/api/matches` to `ai-data-platform`, replacing `matching-engine`.

## 1. Service layout

- **Entry points**
  - `ai-data-platform/api/app.py` — FastAPI read API + match endpoints + dashboard (run: `uvicorn api.app:app`).
  - `ai-data-platform/spine/supervisor.py` — the reconciler/worker that drains the job queue and runs agent stages (`python -m spine.supervisor --drain`).
  - `ai-data-platform/scripts/bootstrap.py` — migration runner + seed loader; `scripts/outreach.py` — enqueues outreach sagas; `scripts/dryrun_match_v2.py` — FPT re-rank into `matches_v2`; `scripts/embed_entities.py` — embedding backfill.
- **Spine modules** (`ai-data-platform/spine/`)
  - `store.py` — all Postgres access (asyncpg pool), job queue, leases, upserts. Key: `list_ready_partners()` at store.py:341.
  - `sagas.py` — onboarding saga (enrich→extract→link) + prompts.
  - `outreach.py` — outreach saga (filter→match→draft→verify) + prompts.
  - `supervisor.py` — scheduler/dispatch, per-stage handlers `_match_stage`/`_draft_stage`/`_verify_stage`.
  - `graph.py` — **GraphRAG** relationship-graph BFS discovery (`RelationshipGraph`, `build_graph`, `find_paths`).
  - `embedding.py` — pgvector query embedding (FPT `/embeddings`, dim 1024, keyless blake2b fallback).
  - `matcher/__init__.py` — `Matcher` Protocol port, `rule_filter`, `normalize_sectors`, `PURPOSE_TYPES`, `_SECTOR_MAP`, `ScoredMatch`.
  - `matcher/llm_judge.py` — `LlmJudgeMatcher` (v1 pi-based rank+explain).
  - `matcher/embedding_matcher.py` — **RAG** pgvector KNN + attribute rescoring (`match()`, `attribute_score()`).
  - `matcher/semantic.py` — standalone reciprocal fit scorer (used by `/discover`).
  - `pools.py`, `transport.py`, `telemetry.py`, `config.py`, `cleanup.py`, `ids.py`, `outreach.py`.
- **Config / data**
  - `agents/specs.yaml` — declarative AgentSpec registry (enricher, extractor, matcher, drafter, verifier, janitor).
  - `schemas/*.json` — R3 output contracts (`enrich, extract, rank, draft, verify, cleanup, enrich`).
  - `migrations/001–004*.sql`; `seed/{startups,investors,corporations,universities}.json`.
  - `dashboard/index.html` — admin dashboard.
- **DB tables**: `entities`, `edges`, `artifacts`, `matches`, `matches_v2` (dry-run, created ad-hoc by `dryrun_match_v2.py`), `jobs`, `events`, `saga_instances`, `workers`, `llm_usage`, `schema_migrations`.

## 2. How a match is triggered end-to-end

There are **two distinct match paths**:

**Path A — Agent outreach saga (writes `matches` table, produces bilingual drafts)**
1. `POST /runs` (api/app.py:658) with `{"startup_id": ...}` or `{"all": true}` → selects `type='startup' AND status='ready'` (app.py:670) → `outreach.start()` (outreach.py:50) creates saga + enqueues `filter` job. API only enqueues; supervisor must be running.
2. Supervisor `_dispatch` (supervisor.py:112) routes by stage:
   - `filter` (code, outreach.py:59): `store.list_ready_partners()` → `rule_filter(startup_view, partners)` (matcher/__init__.py:109). Sector overlap is the only hard gate; capped at `config.MATCH_MAX_CANDIDATES`. Writes a `filter` artifact, fans out a `match` job.
   - `match` (supervisor.py:215): loads candidates from filter artifact → `LlmJudgeMatcher(runner).rank()` (llm_judge.py:87) — one pi call, parses `rank.json` → `store.upsert_match(status='ranked')` → fans out per-partner `draft` jobs.
   - `draft` (supervisor.py:259): `outreach.draft_prompt` → bilingual EN/VI draft → `set_match_draft`.
   - `verify` (supervisor.py:290): `outreach.verify_prompt` LLM-judge; pass → `status='draft_ready'`; fail → re-arm draft with feedback or dead-letter.
3. Results stored in `matches` (migrations/001_core.sql:45). Read via `GET /matches?startup_id=` / `?partner_id=` (app.py:238).

**Path B — RAG match (live frontend path, no agents, reads extract service)**
1. Frontend calls `GET /api/matches/founders/{userId}/investors` or `/api/matches/investors/{userId}/founders` (nginx → ai-data-platform).
2. `matches_founder_to_partners` (app.py:497) / `matches_investor_to_startups` (app.py:509) → `_rag_match_by_user` (app.py:476):
   - `_fetch_extracted(user_id)` (app.py:458) HTTP GETs `EXTRACT_SERVICE_URL/extracted/{user_id}` (the **extract service** — profile source, default `http://extract:3003`).
   - `_norm_from_attrs` maps extract attributes → normalized keys (app.py:441).
   - `embedding_matcher.match()` (embedding_matcher.py:82): `embed_text(query)` → pgvector KNN `ORDER BY embedding <=> $1::vector` over `entities` filtered by `type = ANY(target_types)` → `attribute_score` rescoring, composite = `0.7*vscore + 0.3*attr`; optional LLM `_rerank` adds cited rationale.
   - Returns `{userId, role, matches:[...]}`. **Not persisted** — computed on demand.
3. There are also POST body variants (`/matches/founders/{id}/investors` app.py:402) that take the profile in the body instead of self-fetching.

**GraphRAG path** — `GET /entities/{id}/graph-matches` (app.py:206): builds `RelationshipGraph` from all entities+edges, BFS up to `max_hops`, filter `target_types`. Independent of the `Matcher` port; surfaces relationship-chain discovery.

## 3. WHERE "startup ↔ investor" is hard-coded

This is the core of your extension. Every location that bakes in the two-sided model:

**Entity-type / partner-type constants**
- `api/app.py:39` — `PARTNER_TYPES = ["investor", "corporation", "university", "research_institution"]` — "Everything else is a startup."
- `spine/matcher/__init__.py:25` — `PARTNER_TYPES = ("investor", "corporation", "university", "research_institution")`.
- `spine/store.py:345-346` — `list_ready_partners()` hardcodes `type = ANY(['investor','corporation','university','research_institution'])`. **This is the candidate universe for the outreach saga** — a startup only ever matches these four.
- `migrations/001_core.sql:8` comment — `type` enum listed as `startup | investor | corporation | university | research_institution`.

**Schema columns bake in the two-sidedness**
- `matches` table (`migrations/001_core.sql:45-60`): columns are literally `startup_id` and `partner_id` with `UNIQUE(startup_id, partner_id)` and FKs to `entities`. Same for `matches_v2` (`scripts/dryrun_match_v2.py:32`). Every query in `api/app.py` (`/matches`, `/matches_v2`, `/discover`, `/entities` match_count) and every `store.py` match method (`upsert_match`, `get_match`, `set_match_draft`, `set_match_status`, lines ~355–397) is phrased in `startup_id`/`partner_id`. To go multi-directional you either rename to `subject_id`/`target_id` or overload semantics.

**Purpose → type mapping**
- `spine/matcher/__init__.py:28-35` — `PURPOSE_TYPES`: `funding→investor`, `corporate_pilot/strategic_partnership/market_access→corporation`, `rd_collaboration/talent→university,research_institution`. This is the closest existing seam to "customers/partners/mentors/talents" but it only maps to the 4 partner types (no `customer`, `mentor`, `talent` entity types exist).

**Default target types in endpoints**
- `app.py:416` founder→`["investor"]`; `app.py:433` investor→`["startup"]`; `app.py:506` founder→`PARTNER_TYPES`; `app.py:516` investor→`["startup"]`. All overridable via `?types=` query param (`_target_types`, app.py:376) — so RAG is already partly generalizable, but defaults + route names assume founder/investor.
- `/discover` (app.py:282-302) is hardcoded to `WHERE e.type = 'investor'` and `type = 'startup'`.

**Prompts assume startup↔partner**
- `spine/matcher/llm_judge.py:53-58` — rank prompt: "Partners span four types — investor (funding), corporation (pilots), university & research_institution (R&D)."
- `spine/outreach.py` draft/verify prompts (`_startup_block`, `_partner_block`, `draft_prompt` line 125, `verify_prompt` line 167) — all "STARTUP"/"PARTNER", NIC introduction framing, VN↔world.
- `spine/sagas.py:116` extract prompt `looking_for` enum: `["funding","corporate_pilot","rd_collaboration","talent","market_access","strategic_partnership"]`; `_ALLOWED_KINDS` (sagas.py:35) edge kinds are investment/partnership-flavored.
- `spine/matcher/embedding_matcher.py:176-184` rerank prompt: "why each PARTNER fits this REQUESTER."

**Scoring weights assume investor semantics**
- `embedding_matcher.py:31-78` `attribute_score`: sector Jaccard 0.4, stage 0.3, geo 0.2, **check-size fit** 0.1/0.05 (`funding_ask_usd`/`check_size_usd`, `check_size_min/max_usd`) — capital-specific.
- `matcher/semantic.py:298` `calculate_fit_score`: semantic 40% / sector 20% / **check_size 15%** / stage 10% / geo 5% / behavior 10%; `extract_schema(is_startup)` returns "STARTUP" vs "INVESTOR / PARTNER" (semantic.py:190).

**Extract service (upstream) is strictly two-role**
- `backend/agent/extract/app/routers/extract.py:8` — `ROLES = ("founder", "investor")`; rejects anything else (400).
- `backend/agent/extract/app/db/schema.sql:6` — `role TEXT NOT NULL CHECK (role IN ('founder','investor'))`.
- `backend/agent/extract/app/services/extraction.py:6-7,21` — only `FOUNDER_SCHEMA` / `INVESTOR_SCHEMA`; `build_embedding_text` branches on founder/investor.
- Note: extract embedding is **vector(1536)** (schema.sql:12) while ai-data-platform `entities.embedding` is **vector(1024)** (004_embeddings.sql:9) — two separate corpora/spaces.

## 4. Entity/partner types already in the data model

- `entities.type` is a free-text `TEXT` column (no DB CHECK constraint) — `migrations/001_core.sql:6-14`. So **adding new types requires no migration**; only the hardcoded lists in §3 gate them.
- Seed data present (`seed/*.json`, loaded by `bootstrap.py:55` which flattens each file using its `entity_type` default):
  - `startups.json` — 25 startups (`type:"startup"`, sectors, country, programs).
  - `investors.json` — 20 investors (`hint_website`, `note`).
  - `corporations.json` — 7 (`innovation_areas`).
  - `universities.json` — 5 (`type:"university"`, `research_domains`) — includes universities/research institutions.
  - Total 57 entities. **No** customer / partner-company / mentor / talent seed entities exist yet.
- Frontend already knows 5 entity types with display metadata: `api.js:101-107` `ENTITY_TYPES = {startup, investor, corporation, university, research_institution}` (falls back to a generic "Partner" for unknown types — so unknown new types degrade gracefully).
- `edges.kind` (001_core.sql:24) free-text; produced kinds from `sagas.py:35` `_ALLOWED_KINDS` and traversed kinds in `graph.py:49` `RELATIONAL_KINDS`.

## 5. How the frontend consumes matches

- Views: `frontend/src/views/Matches.jsx`, `frontend/src/views/MatchDetail.jsx`, `frontend/src/views/matches.css`.
- **The multi-direction concept already exists in the UI as `INTENTS`** — `frontend/src/data/ecosystem.js`:
  - `investors` (tab "Investment Fund", **`live: true`**), `customers` ("Potential customers", `live: false`), `partners` ("Partners & mentors", `live: false`), `talent` ("Talent", `live: false`).
  - Matches.jsx:146-150 renders "This match type isn't connected to a backend service yet. Only investor matching is live today." for non-live intents. **This is exactly the switch you'd flip when adding backends for the other directions.**
- Filters (recent-commit "filter by partner type and sector"): Matches.jsx:104-133 — a **Type** chip row built from `typeOptions` (via `entityMeta(t)` labels/dots) and a multi-select **Sector** popover (`SectorSelect`) from `sectorOptions`. Top-K segmented control (5/10/All) at Matches.jsx:138-142.
- API glue: `frontend/src/lib/api.js`
  - `getMatches({userId, role})` (api.js:85) → role `investor` hits `/matches/investors/{id}/founders`, else `/matches/founders/{id}/investors` (`?limit=50`). Two-directional only.
  - `getAllMatches({startupId})` (api.js:94) → gateway `/matches?startup_id=`.
  - `extractProfile(userId)` (api.js:81) → `POST /api/agents/extract/profile` (the extract-then-retry flow).
  - `matchToCandidate(m)` (api.js:122) maps API item `{id,name,type,score,vectorScore,attributeScore,reasons,rationale_en/vi,profile.normalized}` → card shape.

8

10	an LLM). Per R2 it is PERMISSIVE — it must never collapse to zero:
11	  - sector overlap is the only hard gate, and only when BOTH sides carry sectors;
12	    a missing sector signal passes through flagged `low_confidence_filter`.
13	  - purpose (`looking_for`) NEVER hard-drops a partner — a purpose mismatch only sets
14	    `low_confidence_filter`, leaving the LLM judge to down-rank it.
15	Sector tags are matched on a BILINGUAL normalization table so `enterprise_ai` and `ai`,
16	`clean_energy` and `cleantech`, `nông nghiệp` and `agritech` intersect.
17	"""

21	from typing import Protocol, runtime_checkable

31	    "strategic_partnership": {"corporation"},

63	def _canon(tag: str) -> str:
64	    # slugify strips Vietnamese diacritics (đ + NFKD) so "nông nghiệp" -> "nong-nghiep".
65	    t = slugify(str(tag)).replace("-", "_")

111	    unless every partner has a disjoint non-empty sector set.

114	    partner entity dicts must carry id, name, type, profile.
115	    """
116	    s_sectors = normalize_sectors(startup.get("sectors"))
117	    looking_for = [str(x) for x in (startup.get("looking_for") or [])]

124	        p_sectors = _partner_sectors(partner)
125	        # --- sector gate: the ONLY hard drop, and only when both sides have data ---
126	        if s_sectors and p_sectors:
127	            overlap = s_sectors & p_sectors
128	            if not overlap:
129	                continue
130	            sector_lc = False
131	        else:

135	        if not implied:

"""Matcher port (D10, R12) + the deterministic rule-filter (R2).

The `Matcher` protocol makes ranking pluggable: v1 is `LlmJudgeMatcher` (pi ranks +
explains the filtered handful, no embeddings); `EmbeddingMatcher` / `GraphRagMatcher`
drop in later with no saga change (spec §12).

    async def rank(startup, candidates, ctx) -> list[ScoredMatch]

The rule-filter is a cheap deterministic pre-cut that runs in the spine (never calls
an LLM). Per R2 it is PERMISSIVE — it must never collapse to zero:
  - sector overlap is the only hard gate, and only when BOTH sides carry sectors;
    a missing sector signal passes through flagged `low_confidence_filter`.
  - purpose (`looking_for`) NEVER hard-drops a partner — a purpose mismatch only sets
    `low_confidence_filter`, leaving the LLM judge to down-rank it.
Sector tags are matched on a BILINGUAL normalization table so `enterprise_ai` and `ai`,
`clean_energy` and `cleantech`, `nông nghiệp` and `agritech` intersect.
"""
from __future__ import annotations

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Protocol, runtime_checkable

from ..ids import slugify

PARTNER_TYPES = ("investor", "corporation", "university", "research_institution")

# looking_for purpose -> partner types it implies (spec §6 rule-filter / dealflow §1).
PURPOSE_TYPES: dict[str, set[str]] = {
    "funding": {"investor"},
    "corporate_pilot": {"corporation"},
    "strategic_partnership": {"corporation"},
    "market_access": {"corporation"},
    "rd_collaboration": {"university", "research_institution"},
    "talent": {"university", "research_institution"},
}

# Bilingual sector normalization (R2). raw tag -> canonical English tag. Aligned with the
# extract-stage prompt so startup + partner tags land in the same space.
_SECTOR_MAP: dict[str, str] = {
    "enterprise_ai": "ai", "ai_agents": "ai", "automation": "ai", "machine_learning": "ai",
    "ml": "ai", "genai": "ai", "llm": "ai", "ai_ml": "ai", "artificial_intelligence": "ai",
    "tri_tue_nhan_tao": "ai",
    "clean_energy": "cleantech", "cleantech": "cleantech", "renewable_energy": "cleantech",
    "renewables": "cleantech", "thermal_energy_storage": "cleantech",
    "nang_luong_sach": "cleantech",
    "energy": "energy", "smart_grid": "energy", "nang_luong": "energy",
    "agritech": "agritech", "agtech": "agritech", "agriculture": "agritech",
    "smart_agriculture": "agritech", "foodtech": "agritech", "food_tech": "agritech",
    "nong_nghiep": "agritech",
    "healthcare": "healthtech", "biotech": "healthtech", "healthtech": "healthtech",
    "medtech": "healthtech", "life_sciences": "healthtech", "health": "healthtech",
    "y_te": "healthtech",
    "supply_chain": "supply_chain", "logistics": "supply_chain",
    "chuoi_cung_ung": "supply_chain",
    "digital_transformation": "digital_transformation",
    "digital_infrastructure": "digital_transformation",
    "digitalization": "digital_transformation", "dx": "digital_transformation",
    "esg": "sustainability", "sustainability": "sustainability",
    "circular_economy": "sustainability",
}


def _canon(tag: str) -> str:
    # slugify strips Vietnamese diacritics (đ + NFKD) so "nông nghiệp" -> "nong-nghiep".
    t = slugify(str(tag)).replace("-", "_")
    return _SECTOR_MAP.get(t, t)


def normalize_sectors(tags) -> set[str]:
    """Canonicalize a list of raw sector/domain tags to the shared matching space."""
    if not tags:
        return set()
    if isinstance(tags, str):
        tags = [tags]
    return {c for c in (_canon(t) for t in tags) if c}


def _partner_sectors(entity: dict) -> set[str]:
    """Union of a partner's normalized sectors and its seed innovation/research hints."""
    prof = entity.get("profile") or {}
    tags = list((prof.get("normalized") or {}).get("sectors") or [])
    seed = prof.get("seed") or {}
    for key in ("sectors", "innovation_areas", "research_domains"):
        tags += seed.get(key) or []
    return normalize_sectors(tags)


@dataclass(slots=True)
class ScoredMatch:
    partner_id: str
    partner_name: str
    partner_type: str
    composite: float                 # 0-100 fit score
    rationale_en: str
    rationale_vi: str
    semantic: float | None = None    # 0-1 (optional, LLM self-report)
    sector_overlap: float | None = None


@runtime_checkable
class Matcher(Protocol):
    """Pluggable ranking port (R12). Ranks a pre-filtered candidate handful for one
    startup and returns explained ScoredMatch rows, best first."""

    async def rank(self, startup: dict, candidates: list[dict],
                   ctx: dict) -> list[ScoredMatch]: ...


def rule_filter(startup: dict, partners: list[dict]) -> list[dict]:
    """Deterministic permissive pre-cut (R2). Returns candidate dicts, never empty
    unless every partner has a disjoint non-empty sector set.

    startup: {"sectors": [...], "looking_for": [...], "stage": str|None}
    partner entity dicts must carry id, name, type, profile.
    """
    s_sectors = normalize_sectors(startup.get("sectors"))
    looking_for = [str(x) for x in (startup.get("looking_for") or [])]
    implied: set[str] = set()
    for p in looking_for:
        implied |= PURPOSE_TYPES.get(p, set())

    out: list[dict] = []
    for partner in partners:
        p_sectors = _partner_sectors(partner)
        # --- sector gate: the ONLY hard drop, and only when both sides have data ---
        if s_sectors and p_sectors:
            overlap = s_sectors & p_sectors
            if not overlap:
                continue
            sector_lc = False
        else:
            overlap = set()
            sector_lc = True  # missing signal -> pass through, low confidence
        # --- purpose: never drops; a mismatch only flags low confidence ---
        if not implied:
            purpose_lc = True
        else:
            purpose_lc = partner["type"] not in implied
        out.append({
            "partner_id": partner["id"],
            "partner_name": partner["name"],
            "partner_type": partner["type"],
            "sector_overlap": sorted(overlap),
            "purpose_match": bool(implied) and not purpose_lc,
            "low_confidence_filter": sector_lc or purpose_lc,
        })
    return out

file.content:
"""FastAPI read API + dashboard (spec §8, §13, Phase 3).

Read-only projections over Postgres for the dashboard, plus POST /runs to trigger
an outreach run. Endpoints (spec §8): /entities /matches /sagas /jobs /events
/workers /metrics /costs + POST /runs. The supervisor (python -m spine.supervisor
--drain) drains any jobs this enqueues; the API itself never touches agents.

Run: uv run uvicorn api.app:app --port 8000
"""
from __future__ import annotations

import asyncio
import base64
import hashlib
import hmac
import json
import os
import re
import time
import uuid
from contextlib import asynccontextmanager
from decimal import Decimal
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Any

from fastapi import FastAPI, HTTPException, Query, Request
from fastapi.middleware.cors import CORSMiddleware
from fastapi.responses import FileResponse, JSONResponse, RedirectResponse

from spine import outreach, telemetry
from spine.graph import build_graph, path_to_dict
from spine.matcher import embedding_matcher
from spine.store import Store

log = telemetry.get_logger("api")
_DASHBOARD = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent / "dashboard" / "index.html"

# Partner entity types (spec §5.1). Everything else is a startup.
PARTNER_TYPES = ["investor", "corporation", "university", "research_institution"]

# User profiles live in the separate backend DB; the platform fetches them from the
# extract service over HTTP (GET /extracted/:userId) for the by-user /matches routes.
EXTRACT_SERVICE_URL = os.environ.get("EXTRACT_SERVICE_URL", "http://extract:3003").rstrip("/")
_QUERY_WORDS = re.compile(r"[a-z0-9][a-z0-9_-]{1,}", re.I)


@asynccontextmanager
async def lifespan(app: FastAPI):
    telemetry.configure()
    app.state.store = await Store.connect(min_size=1, max_size=5)
    log.info("api_started")
    try:
        yield
    finally:
        await app.state.store.close()


app = FastAPI(title="Deal-flow Matchmaker", lifespan=lifespan)

# Permissive CORS: read-only API, no cookie auth — wildcard origin is safe only with
# allow_credentials=False (browsers reject "*" + credentials).
app.add_middleware(
    CORSMiddleware,
    allow_origins=["*"],
    allow_methods=["*"],
    allow_headers=["*"],
    allow_credentials=False,
)


def _pool(app: FastAPI):
    return app.state.store.pool


def _jsonable(v: Any) -> Any:
    """Normalize asyncpg row values: Decimal -> float, jsonb text -> parsed."""
    if isinstance(v, Decimal):
        return float(v)
    return v


def _row(record) -> dict:
    return {k: _jsonable(v) for k, v in dict(record).items()}


def _parse_jsonb(d: dict, *fields: str) -> dict:
    for f in fields:
        if isinstance(d.get(f), str):
            try:
                d[f] = json.loads(d[f])
            except (json.JSONDecodeError, TypeError):
                pass
    return d


# --------------------------------------------------------------------------- #
# dashboard — admin-only when JWT_SECRET is set (gateway-issued HS256 tokens).
# Admins exist only via direct DB update (UPDATE users SET role='admin'); the
# gateway never registers them. Data endpoints below stay public — the frontend
# match flow reads /matches directly.
# --------------------------------------------------------------------------- #

_JWT_SECRET = [REDACTED]("JWT_SECRET", "")
_ADMIN_COOKIE = "vn_admin"


def _b64url_decode(seg: str) -> bytes:
    return base64.urlsafe_b64decode(seg + "=" * (-len(seg) % 4))


def _verify_admin_jwt(token: str) -> bool:
    """Verify a gateway HS256 JWT and require role=admin. stdlib-only on purpose:
    this service otherwise has no JWT dependency."""
    try:
        header_b64, payload_b64, sig_b64 = token.split(".")
        if json.loads(_b64url_decode(header_b64)).get("alg") != "HS256":
            return False
        expected = hmac.new(
            _JWT_SECRET.encode(), f"{header_b64}.{payload_b64}".encode(), hashlib.sha256
        ).digest()

180	    row = await pool.fetchrow(
181	        "SELECT id, type, name, status, profile, created_at, updated_at "
182	        "FROM entities WHERE id = $1",
183	        entity_id,

185	    if row is None:
186	        raise HTTPException(404, "entity not found")
187	    # Relationship graph (§5.1 edges): outgoing from this entity + incoming resolved ones,
188	    # with the counterpart's display name when it has been onboarded.
189	    edge_rows = await pool.fetch(
190	        "SELECT e.src_id, e.dst_id, e.dst_name, e.dst_resolved, e.kind, e.source_url, "
191	        "s.name AS src_name, d.name AS dst_entity_name "
192	        "FROM edges e LEFT JOIN entities s ON s.id = e.src_id "
193	        "LEFT JOIN entities d ON d.id = e.dst_id AND e.dst_resolved "
194	        "WHERE e.src_id = $1 OR (e.dst_id = $1 AND e.dst_resolved) "
195	        "ORDER BY e.kind",
196	        entity_id,
197	    )
198	    out = _parse_jsonb(_row(row), "profile")
199	    out["edges"] = [
200	        {**_row(r), "direction": "out" if r["src_id"] == entity_id else "in"}
201	        for r in edge_rows
202	    ]
203	    return out

206	@app.get("/entities/{entity_id}/graph-matches")
207	async def graph_matches(entity_id: str, max_hops: int = Query(2, ge=1, le=3),
208	                        types: str | None = Query(
209	                            None,
210	                            description="Comma-separated target types, e.g. "
211	                                        "'investor,corporation'. Default: all partner types.")):
212	    """Relationship-graph discovery (spec §5.1: edges "consumed by future GraphRAG").
213	
214	    Unlike GET /entities/{id}'s `edges` list (which only shows edges whose dst_id was
215	    already resolved at write time — rare in practice), this re-resolves edge targets by
216	    name against the full current entity set and BFS-traverses up to `max_hops`. Answers
217	    "which investors/corporations is this startup N hops from via a real relationship"
218	    (shared funding rounds, partnerships, pilots) — a signal the LlmJudgeMatcher never
219	    sees, since it only looks at one startup + its filtered candidate list at a time.
220	    """
221	    pool = _pool(app)
222	    if await pool.fetchval("SELECT 1 FROM entities WHERE id = $1", entity_id) is None:
223	        raise HTTPException(404, "entity not found")
224	
225	    entity_rows = await pool.fetch("SELECT id, type, name FROM entities")
226	    edge_rows = await pool.fetch("SELECT src_id, dst_id, dst_name, kind, source_url FROM edges")
227	
228	    graph = build_graph([dict(r) for r in entity_rows], [dict(r) for r in edge_rows])
229	    target_types = set(t.strip() for t in types.split(",") if t.strip()) if types else None
230	    paths = graph.find_paths(entity_id, max_hops=max_hops, target_types=target_types)
231	    return {"entity_id": entity_id, "max_hops": max_hops, "matches": [path_to_dict(p) for p in paths]}
